result.markdown:
--- title: "Conway's Blobs" jupyter: brainvomit execute: echo: false warning: false --- Inspired by Conway's *Game of Life*, I wanted to extend the experiment to simulate not only the rules but also the game-theoretic consequences of what those rules imply, given a population of agents that can sense and interact with the universe and each other. BlobSim is something between a cellular automaton and simple ecological sandbox. It is a small, toroidal gridded world. Each cell can hold one **blob** — a simple creature with energy and metabolism. Time advances in discrete steps. At each step, every blob inspects its surroundings, selects an action (move, eat, reproduce, or rest) based on a swappable 'intelligence engine', and the universe runs the energy accounting/conflict resolution via asserted laws. Run out of energy and you die; eat well and you reproduce. The grid itself generates food at a configurable rate. You can imagine how BlobSim arises from the Game of Life via abstraction of just two key ideas: 1. Agent state is defined not as a binary dead/alive, but as a continuous energy level. 2. Conway's behavioral rules are a function of the local neighborhood; BlobSim introduces probabilistic decision-making based on the underlying swappable 'engine'. ie BlobSim is reducible to Conway's Game of Life if the engine is deterministic and the energy level is binary. ## Design The grid wraps around at the edges (a blob walking off the right side reappears on the left). Every step runs the same fixed sequence: 1. observe: each blob gathers local information 2. decide: engines choose an action based on observations and internal state 3. conflict resolution: since time steps are discrete, the ordering of actions must be resolved 4. energy accounting/execution: conservation laws are enforced, and the energy ledger is updated; resolved actions are applied to the grid and the blobs 5. grid update: the environment adds energy to the grid according to its rules The behaviour of a blob (the "engine"), the behaviour of the environment (decaying vs. replenishing), and how conflicts are resolved are each swappable without touching the rest. ## Universal laws A set of invariants about this world are asserted, to maintain a somewhat coherent and internally consistent universe: - **Conservation of energy.** At any moment, the energy held by living blobs, plus the energy left in the grid, plus everything spent so far, equals what the world started with (plus anything the environment has since added) - **Reproduction and predation only moves energy.** A new blob is funded entirely out of its parent's energy; a predator's kill transfers energy from the victim to the attacker - **Death is permanent.** A blob whose energy falls to the death threshold is removed forever - **Determinism.** The same starting seed reproduces the exact same run - **One blob per cell.** No two blobs ever occupy the same square. ## Example A finite grid with no replenishment can only end one way — the blobs eat through the available energy and die out: ![Two kinds of blob draw down a finite grid until none are left.](/assets/images/blobsim/sc1_decay_extinction.gif) One run's population and total energy over time: ::: {#cell-live-sim .cell execution_count=2} ::: {#live-sim .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: The run opens with a boom — the grazers find food and grow ~10x in ~20 steps, overshooting the grid's energy capacity. At that inflection point, the population draws energy down faster than it regenerates, so the energy level drops and the population crashes. The two mechanisms settle into a gradually dampening feedback loop that behaves as out-of-phase swings, converging toward the sustainable stready-state population. ## Benchmark runs/base cases For purpose of sanity checking, each base case below computes a target *analytically from the rules* (a conservation identity, an expected lifespan, a stable population) and then checks the simulation derives it within tolerance. ### Predicted lifespan **The rule.** A lone blob lives in two phases: an *active* phase where it can still afford to move (paying a higher average cost per step) and a *resting* phase, below that affordability threshold, where it can only idle at the fixed metabolic cost until it starves. Adding the expected length of both phases gives a closed form, $$E[\tau] \;=\; \frac{E_0 - B}{E[c]} \;+\; \frac{B}{M} \;+\; (\text{boundary correction}),$$ with starting energy $E_0$, resting cost $M$, average active cost $E[c]$, and the affordability threshold $B$ below which moving is no longer possible. For the test parameters this predicts **≈ 186 steps**, and the simulation agrees. But one match could be luck, so I varied the starting energy and the movement cost across ten settings and compared each one's *predicted* lifespan to its *observed* mean. Every point lands on the $y = x$ line — the formula tracks the simulation across the whole range, not just at one spot: ![Observed mean lifespan vs. the closed-form prediction, across ten parameter settings (20 runs averaged per point). Every point sits on the dashed y=x line, so the formula holds across conditions — not a one-off coincidence.](/assets/images/blobsim/bench_lifespan.png) ### Guaranteed extinction on a finite grid **The rule.** If the environment adds no energy, then every step at least some energy is spent and never returned, so the world's total can only fall. With a finite starting amount $E_0$ and a smallest per-step metabolic cost $m$, every blob must die within $$T_\text{max} = \left\lceil E_0 / m \right\rceil$$ steps — and reproduction can't postpone it, because offspring are funded from parents (energy only moves, it isn't created). $T_\text{max}=E_0/m$ is a **ceiling**: it's how long the energy lasts if every joule goes to the cheapest possible upkeep — the metabolic cost $m$ per step. A blob that only *rests* spends exactly that, so it hits the ceiling precisely; a blob that also *moves* spends more and dies sooner. Sweeping $E_0$ and $m$, the resters land right on the line and the movers fall below it — extinction always lands *within* $T_\text{max}$, never past it: ![Observed extinction step vs. the ceiling T = E₀/m. Blobs that only rest spend exactly m/step and hit the ceiling (purple, on the line); blobs that also move spend more and die sooner (orange, below). Nothing reaches the grey zone above the line — extinction is always within the bound.](/assets/images/blobsim/bench_extinction_time.png) Add foraging and reproduction and $T_\text{max}$ loosens into an upper bound (blobs can't share energy, so they die *sooner* than the worst case), but extinction stays *guaranteed*: with no source the world's total energy only ever falls (left below), so even a population that *booms* first (right) is only reshuffling a shrinking pool — once it's drawn down, everyone starves. ![Left: total energy (blobs + grid) over eight runs — it only ever falls, since nothing replenishes it. Right: population booms to ~390, then crashes to zero by ~step 35. Reproduction redistributes energy but can't create it.](/assets/images/blobsim/bench_extinction.png) ### Propagation, ceiling, then death **The rule.** Give blobs a rich but *non-replenishing* grid and they multiply fast — but this is not a *cycle*, it's a one-way trip: **propagation → ceiling → death**. The population spreads until it hits a ceiling set by *space*, not energy (with one blob per cell it can't exceed the number of cells); then, with no new energy coming in, the crowd draws the finite store down and starves all at once. There is no recovery — the energy to fund a second wave was already spent. ![A population spreads to fill the grid, hits the one-per-cell ceiling, then starves to extinction.](/assets/images/blobsim/sc4_boom_bust.gif) ### Carrying capacity **The rule.** When the grid replenishes, the population settles where energy coming in balances energy spent — so there's a single *carrying capacity* the world returns to from any starting density. Balancing the two flows gives a back-of-the-envelope estimate $$N^* \approx \frac{\text{rate}\times(\text{cells})}{\text{rate} + \text{avg cost per blob}} \approx 106,$$ but this one is *not* exact like the lifespan formula above — it's a mean-field guess that comes in ~25% low (more on why below). The claim worth testing is the **attractor**: crowded and sparse starts should both end up at the same level, wherever that level is. ::: {layout-ncol=2} ![Sparse start — grows toward the steady level.](/assets/images/blobsim/sc5_sparse.gif) ![Crowded start — relaxes down to the same level.](/assets/images/blobsim/sc5_dense.gif) ::: To show it's a genuine *attractor* — a level the world returns to no matter where it starts — I swept the starting population from 10 to 200 and measured where each run settles (mean of its last 300 steps). The result is flat: every start lands near the same equilibrium (~133), nowhere near the dotted $y = x$ line a "stays where it began" world would follow. That flatness is the carrying capacity, and it sits near **133**, not 106. The gap is the formula's one shortcut: it assumes a blob pays the move cost almost every step (the $\tfrac{8}{9}$ in "avg cost per blob"). Real blobs idle far more — resting when low on energy, or when no move helps — so their true upkeep is lower, and lower upkeep supports more of them. The mean-field gets the *scale* right; it's the assumed move rate that makes the exact number ~25% low. ![Equilibrium population vs. starting population. From starts spanning 10–200, every run settles near one level (~133) — far off the y=x line — which is what makes it an attractor. The mean-field estimate N* ≈ 106 (red dashed) runs ~25% low.](/assets/images/blobsim/bench_carrying.png) ### A lone blob's energy **The rule.** A single blob on a fast-replenishing grid absorbs more each step than it spends, so its energy climbs quickly to the maximum it can hold and then stays pinned there — steady, with essentially no variation. Several runs, same story every time: ![Energy of a lone blob across several runs — each climbs to the maximum it can hold (here 60) within ~15 steps and stays pinned.](/assets/images/blobsim/bench_lone_energy.png) ### Extinction threshold **The rule.** Whether a population survives hinges on how fast the grid replenishes. There is a **critical rate**: below it, each blob can't absorb enough to replace what it spends, so on average it leaves fewer than one surviving offspring and the line dies out; above it, survival becomes possible. The crossover is sharp — a phase transition. ::: {layout-ncol=2} ![Below the critical rate — the population dies out.](/assets/images/blobsim/c2_extinction.gif){width="320"} ![Above the critical rate — the population survives.](/assets/images/blobsim/c2_survival.gif){width="320"} ::: Sweeping the rate and measuring how often the population survives (10 seeds per rate) draws out the transition: survival jumps from near-zero to near-certain across a narrow band around the critical rate ≈ 0.075. ![Survival probability versus grid replenishment rate. The sharp rise across ~0.075 is the phase transition.](/assets/images/blobsim/bench_threshold.png) ### Grazers vs. walkers **The rule.** Put two strategies on one grid: undirected **random walkers** and **grazers** that move toward the richest neighbouring cell. With identical metabolism, the grazers absorb more per step, so they reproduce faster and eventually take over. Greater efficiency compounds into dominance. ![Energy-seeking grazers outcompete random walkers.](/assets/images/blobsim/c3_competition.gif) ## Emergent behavior My primary interest was in discovering non-obvious emergence. Coexistence niches, non-obvious optimization strategies, and other patterns that arise from the interaction of simple rules. ### Predation To introduce direct inter-species interactions, a blob can now **attack** an adjacent blob of a species it preys on. Hunt success is determined by energy: the attacker wins only if it currently holds *more* energy than its victim; a kill transfers the victim's energy to the attacker; a failed hunt costs the attempt. Crucially, predators stay **omnivores**: every blob still grazes the grid as before; hunting is purely opportunistic. ### The ouroboros I discovered the conditions to produce an ouroborous: A eating B in one place while B eats A in another, the dividing line forever circling. The missing ingredient is a **cycle of three**: A eats B, B eats C, C eats A — rock, paper, scissors. Now no one is strongest, and from a random scatter the grid spontaneously sorts itself into travelling patches that chase each other forever — but only when the world is big enough. Coexistence is a *spatial* effect: the three survive because the rotating patches keep them apart. Shrink the grid and a single chance extinction cascades — one species loses its only predator, booms, and wipes out the third — collapsing the cycle to a single colour. (Below ~20 cells a side it almost always collapses; past ~36 it reliably survives — quantified [below](#how-big-must-the-world-be).) ::: {layout-ncol=2} ![Big world (60×60): the three sort into rotating domains, each colour endlessly eating into the next and eaten from behind.](/assets/images/blobsim/cyclic_ouroboros.gif){width="300"} ![Small world (22×22): a chance fluctuation kills one species, the cascade fires, and only one colour survives.](/assets/images/blobsim/cyclic_collapse.gif){width="300"} ::: ![The cyclic world at five steps — domains advance and retreat as each colour eats into the one it dominates.](/assets/images/blobsim/cyclic_frames.png) The population never converges: each population oscillates, peaking after the species it eats, all three locked in the same rotation. Two views of that rotation — the populations over time (left), and the same whole-grid A:B:C mix drawn step by step as a path on a triangle (right), where each corner is "all one species", the centre an even split, and colour runs dark (start) → bright (end): ::: {layout-ncol=2} ![Populations over the run — sustained, out-of-phase oscillation; each peak chases the prey it eats.](/assets/images/blobsim/cyclic_populations.png) ![The whole-grid mix drawn as a path, orbiting the three-way centre — never reaching a corner or settling.](/assets/images/blobsim/cyclic_orbit.gif){width="240"} ::: The system never diverges into corners (no species ever wins) and never reaches the centre (convergence) — its population mix is in perpetual orbit. This universe cycles ~ 0.5 million units of energy through births, kills, and metabolism over 1.5k steps, and the conservation ledger still balances to machine precision. ### How big must the world be? That collapse in a small world isn't arbitrary — it has a length scale. Coexistence is a spatial trick: the three only survive because the rotating domains keep them apart, so the grid has to be big enough to *hold* a full domain. Below that, a chance fluctuation wipes out one species before the pattern can stabilise, and the cascade finishes the rest. So the prediction is that the minimum grid size is set by the **domain wavelength** $\lambda$ — the natural width of one rotating patch. I measured $\lambda$ independently, from the spatial autocorrelation of a single species on a large (70×70) grid: $\lambda \approx 33$ cells. Then I swept the grid size and measured how often all three survive: ![Survival vs. grid size (10 seeds each). Coexistence is near-impossible below ~20, turns on through the mid-20s, and is reliable past ~36. The independently-measured wavelength λ ≈ 33 (blue dashed) lands right where coexistence becomes reliable.](/assets/images/blobsim/cyclic_finite_size.png){width="460"} The match is real but soft: $\lambda \approx 33$ lands at the top of the transition, where survival crosses into reliable — not at a single razor edge. That's expected. Unlike $E_0/m$ or $N^*$, this threshold isn't a closed form in the parameters; it's an *emergent* scale, and the transition is a probabilistic band several cells wide, not a step. But the headline holds: the world has to be about one spiral wide for the ouroboros to survive, and a length measured from the pattern *itself* predicts where that happens. (This is the lattice version of Reichenbach et al., *Nature* 2007 — coexistence needs the system larger than a spiral wavelength.)
